serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre serving is approximately one bowl weighing 500g, comprising clear or milky fish broth, approximately 120g of deep-fried batang fish slices, rice vermicelli (bee hoon), tomato slices, and garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Fish Slice Bee Hoon Soup — HPB Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Fish Slice Bee Hoon Soup (F03020014). All per-100g values are taken from this verified HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli / bee hoon reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. peer-reviewed GI tables
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) is assigned based on the GI of rice vermicelli (bee hoon), the primary carbohydrate source in this dish. This is consistent with the GI applied to the analogous steamed fish bee hoon soup (HPB ID 558). Glycemic load of 26.1 per 500g bowl is calculated as: (GI 58 × carbohydrate per serving 45g) / 100 = 26.1. The deep-fried fish component adds fat that may modestly attenuate the glycaemic response but does not materially alter the GI classification. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 650mg per 500g bowl from broth and soy seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume this dish in moderation; sodium per bowl (~650mg) represents approximately 28% of the 2300mg daily limit, and requesting less broth or reduced seasoning can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load of 26.1 per bowl driven by rice vermicelli (bee hoon); fat from deep-frying modestly attenuates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the GL of ~26.1 per bowl is moderate-to-high; portion control of the bee hoon component and choosing a smaller serving can reduce postprandial glucose excursion, and this dish is preferable to higher-GL hawker options such as laksa or char kway teow.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (2.5g per bowl), moderate omega-3 from batang fish, and no trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is relatively heart-friendly for a hawker meal given its low saturated fat content (~2.5g per bowl), absence of trans fat, and moderate omega-3 contribution from batang (Spanish mackerel); however, the sodium level warrants attention for patients with concurrent hypertension and CVD.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate omega-3 from batang fish; predominantly MUFA/PUFA frying oil; low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The batang fish provides moderate omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties, and the low saturated fat profile makes this dish acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis, though the frying oil type at individual stalls may vary.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish Slice Bee Hoon Soup is a popular Singapore hawker dish of Teochew-Cantonese origin. It is the deep-fried variant of the classic steamed fish bee hoon soup. Batang fish (Spanish mackerel) slices are coated lightly and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then placed in a bowl over rice vermicelli (bee hoon). Hot clear or milky fish broth is ladled over the fish, softening the flesh while preserving crispy edges. The dish is typically garnished with tomato slices, spring onion, ginger, and fried shallots. Compared to the steamed version, the frying step adds approximately 8–10g of absorbed fat per serving, raising the calorie count from approximately 325 kcal (steamed) to approximately 390–420 kcal per 500g bowl. Per 100g, the dish provides 78 kcal, 5.5g protein, 2.4g fat, and 9.0g carbohydrate. Sodium is approximately 130mg per 100g (650mg per bowl). The glycemic load per bowl is approximately 26.1, driven by the rice vermicelli. Clinically, this dish is among the better hawker choices for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease compared to high-fat broth dishes such as laksa or prawn mee. The steamed variant remains nutritionally superior for calorie and fat-conscious patients, but the fried variant offers a more satisfying texture and is still a reasonable hawker meal choice when consumed in appropriate portions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (verified, canonical entry F03020014). GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli component using the University of Sydney GI Database as the analogous reference, consistent with HPB methodology for similar dishes. All mandatory multilingual names are present for a Singaporean dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Singapore hawker dish is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB verified source). GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (bee hoon) as the primary carbohydrate source, consistent with HPB methodology for steamed fish bee hoon soup (ID 558); estimation basis documented in gi_source. Glycemic load per 500g bowl calculated as (58 × 45g carbohydrate) / 100 = 26.1. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Vitamin A reported as 6.0 mcg (assumed RAE equivalent from HPB source). Folate reported as 6.0 mcg (assumed DFE equivalent from HPB source). country_of_origin and region fields in the input cultural object contained narrative text rather than proper values; corrected to 'Singapore' in this output.
